macro_segments.title: Introduction to Deathbed Gift (Matnat Shachiv Mera) and the Principle of Interpreting Language as Gift Rather Than Inheritance

micro_segments.title: Overview of Matnat Shachiv Mera and Rabbinic Latitude in Redefining Inheritance
Mishna's Principle: Reading Declarations as Gift (Matana) Rather Than Inheritance (Yerusha) When the Word Matana Appears Anywhere

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Examples: When the Word Matana (Gift) Appears at Beginning, Middle, or End of Declaration

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Teaching from Rabbi Yochanan: Matana at the Beginning, Middle, or End All Work
Restriction: Same Person and Same Field—When Matana Language Applies to Everything
Different People or Different Fields: The Word Matana Only Applies to What It Directly References

macro_segments.title: Three Amoraic Positions on Borrowing Matana Language Across Different People and Fields

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Version of Rabbi Yochanan: Same Person AND Same Field Required
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Either Same Person OR Same Field Suffices; Different Person AND Different Field Fails
Ravin's Version of Rabbi Yochanan: Even Different Person AND Different Field Works
Abaye's Challenge: The Two Versions of Rabbi Yochanan Are Contradictory; Rabbi Eliezer's Versions Are Complementary

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Position: Two People and Two Fields Requires Tight Packaging of Language

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish: Different People and Different Fields Requires Explicit Formula Packaging Gift and Inheritance Language Together

macro_segments.title: Rav Hamnuna, Rav Nachman, and Rav Sheshes: Parallel Positions in Earlier Discussion

micro_segments.title: Rav Hamnuna: Only Same Person and Same Field
Rav Nachman: Either Same Person or Same Field; Two People and Two Fields Fails
Rav Sheshes: Even Two People and Two Fields Works—Borrowing Matana Language Across

macro_segments.title: Rav Sheshes's Proof from Braisa: Father Setting Aside Fund for Children with Remainder to Others

micro_segments.title: Braisa: Person Instructs to Give Children Weekly Allowance, With Remainder to Reuven and Shimon After Children Die
Interpretation of Shekel Amount: Executor Latitude When Children Inherit Balance vs. Exactness When Others Inherit Remainder
Conditional Inheritance: Language of Inheritance (Yerusha) Works Here Because Matana Language for Children Applies to Others as Well

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Proof: Successor-in-Line Formula (Nechsav Lecha, Ve-Acharecha Le-Ploni) with Sequential Inheritance

micro_segments.title: Three-Way Successive Gift: First Person Gets Property, Then Second, Then Third Person—All via Yerusha Language After First
Effect of Death Out of Sequence: If Second Heir Dies Before First, Property Reverts to First's Heirs—Chain Cannot Skip

macro_segments.title: Refutation of Rabbi Yochanan ben Broka Explanation: Why Matana Language (Not Yerusha via Inheritance) Must Be the Mechanism

micro_segments.title: Potential Defense: Perhaps Yerusha Language Works Because Second and Third Beneficiaries Are Heirs, Citing Rabbi Yochanan ben Broka's Allowance of Reassigning Yerusha Among Heirs
Rav Acha bar Rav Adda's Refutation: Under RYbB, Once First Heir Designated, Second Heir Gets Nothing—Inheritance Does Not Interrupt (Yerusha Ein La Hefsek)
Conclusion: Since Second Heir Can Get It After First Heir Dies, We Are NOT Following RYbB; We Must Be Borrowing Matana Language Even Across Different People and Fields

macro_segments.title: Resolution: Borrowing Matana Language Works Only Within Toch Kedei Dibbur (Time of One Statement); Different Time Requires Reish Lakish's Tight Packaging

micro_segments.title: Apparent Contradiction: How Can Rava Rule Like Reish Lakish (Requiring Tight Packaging for Two People and Two Fields) When Proof Shows It Works via Matana Borrowing?
Resolution: Reish Lakish Applies When Statements Are Separated by More Than Toch Kedei Dibbur; Within Toch Kedei Dibbur, Statements Are One Unit and Matana Borrowing Works
General Principle: Toch Kedei Dibbur = One Statement, Except in Cases of Avoda Zara, Kiddushin, and Get (Where Retractions Cannot Occur)

macro_segments.title: Explanation of Toch Kedei Dibbur Concept: Rabbeinu Tam's Logic and Rashbam's Analysis of When One Word Interprets Another

micro_segments.title: Rabbeinu Tam: Toch Kedei Dibbur Instituted to Avoid Rudeness—Allow Time to Greet a Passing Rabbi Without Losing a Business Deal
Rashbam's Broader Analysis: Toch Kedei Dibbur Covers Multiple Scenarios—Both Statements Possible, One Interprets Another, or Two Contradictory Statements
In Our Case: Yerusha Language Has No Halachic Effect; Matana Language Does—So Matana Dominates and Interprets Yerusha When Within Toch Kedei Dibbur

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yochanan (Rav Dimi's version vs. Ravin's version)
Same person and same field (Adam echad ve-sadeh achat)
Different people and different fields (Shnei benei adam u-shtei sadot)
One person and two fields (Adam echad u-shtei sadot)
One field and two people (Sadeh achat u-shnei benei adam)
Toch Kedei Dibbur (Time it takes to say a greeting)
Yerusha Ein La Hefsek (Inheritance Does Not Interrupt)
Avoda Zara (Idolatry—Exception to Toch Kedei Dibbur)
Geirushin (Divorce Document—Exception to Toch Kedei Dibbur)
